text: So far in the book, we have studied properties of numbers (whether a number is odd, positive, prime; whether one number is divisible by another) and operations on numbers (addition, greatest common divisor).
html: So far in the book, we have studied properties of numbers (whether a number is odd, positive, prime; whether one number is divisible by another) and operations on numbers (addition, greatest common divisor).

text: In this chapter we go up a level of abstraction, and study properties of and operations on functions. These new properties include: whether a function is injective , surjective , bijective ; whether one function is inverse to another; and the operation of composition .
html: In this chapter we go up a level of abstraction, and study properties of and operations on functions. These new properties include: whether a function is injective, surjective, bijective; whether one function is inverse to another; and the operation of composition.

text: We also expand our horizon beyond the numeric types ( \(\mathbb{N}\) , \(\mathbb{Z}\) , \(\mathbb{Q}\) , \(\mathbb{R}\) ) which have formed the setting of the early part of the book. We now start to work with a broader range of types, including function types, finite inductive types, and product types.
html: We also expand our horizon beyond the numeric types (\(\mathbb{N}\), \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(\mathbb{Q}\), \(\mathbb{R}\)) which have formed the setting of the early part of the book. We now start to work with a broader range of types, including function types, finite inductive types, and product types.

text: We have studied particular functions before. For example, the Fibonacci sequence from Example 6.3.3 is a function from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\) : it takes in a natural number, for example 5, and gives out an integer, in this case 8 (the index-5 term of the sequence).
html: We have studied particular functions before. For example, the Fibonacci sequence from Example 6.3.3 is a function from \(\mathbb{N}\) to \(\mathbb{Z}\): it takes in a natural number, for example 5, and gives out an integer, in this case 8 (the index-5 term of the sequence).

text: The domain of a function is the type where it takes its input values, and the codomain of a function is the type where it takes its output values. For example, the domain of the Fibonacci sequence is \(\mathbb{N}\) and its codomain is \(\mathbb{Z}\) . The type of functions with domain \(\mathbb{N}\) and codomain \(\mathbb{Z}\) is denoted \(\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{Z}\) . Lean will confirm for us that the Fibonacci sequence F has this type:

text: A function \(f : X \to Y\) is injective , if for all \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) of type \(X\) , if \(f(x_1)=f(x_2)\) , then \(x_1=x_2\) .

text: Theorem
html: Theorem

text: A function \(f:X\to Y\) is bijective, if and only if for all \(y\) of type \(Y\) , there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\) , such that \(f(x)=y\) .

text: Proof
html: Proof

text: First, suppose that \(f\) is bijective. Let \(y\) be of type \(Y\) . Since \(f\) is surjective, there exists \(x\) of type \(X\) , such that \(f(x)=y\) . We will show that this \(x\) is unique. Indeed, for any other \(x'\) with \(f(x')=y\) , we have that
html: First, suppose that \(f\) is bijective. Let \(y\) be of type \(Y\). Since \(f\) is surjective, there exists \(x\) of type \(X\), such that \(f(x)=y\). We will show that this \(x\) is unique. Indeed, for any other \(x'\) with \(f(x')=y\), we have that

text: and so by the injectivity of \(f\) , \(x'=x\) .

text: Conversely, suppose that for all \(y\) of type \(Y\) , there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\) , such that \(f(x)=y\) . ( \(\star\) ) We must show that \(f\) is injective and surjective.
html: Conversely, suppose that for all \(y\) of type \(Y\), there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\), such that \(f(x)=y\). (\(\star\)) We must show that \(f\) is injective and surjective.

text: For the injectivity, let \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) be of type \(X\) , and suppose that \(f(x_1)=f(x_2)\) . By ( \(\star\) ) applied with \(y=f(x_1)\) , there exists a unique \(x\) of type \(X\) for which \(f(x)=f(x_1)\) . So, by the uniqueness, \(x_1=x\) , and also, since \(f(x_2)=f(x_1)=f(x)\) , \(x_2=x\) . Combining these, \(x_1=x_2\) .

text: Problem

text: Show that it is not true that for all functions \(f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}\) , if \(f\) is injective then it is bijective.

text: Solution
html: Solution

text: We must show that there exists a function \(f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}\) , which is injective but not bijective.
html: We must show that there exists a function \(f:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{N}\), which is injective but not bijective.

text: Indeed, consider the function \(f(n)=n+1\) . This function is injective, since for all natural numbers \(n_1\) and \(n_2\) , if \(n_1+1=n_2+1\) then \(n_1=n_2\) .
html: Indeed, consider the function \(f(n)=n+1\). This function is injective, since for all natural numbers \(n_1\) and \(n_2\), if \(n_1+1=n_2+1\) then \(n_1=n_2\).

text: However, this function is not surjective, and therefore not bijective. To see this, observe that for all natural numbers \(n\) , we have \(f(n)=n+1>0\) and therefore \(f(n)\ne 0\) .
html: However, this function is not surjective, and therefore not bijective. To see this, observe that for all natural numbers \(n\), we have \(f(n)=n+1>0\) and therefore \(f(n)\ne 0\).

text: The composition of the function \(g : Y \to Z\) with the function \(f : X \to Y\) is the function from \(X\) to \(Z\) which sends \(x\) to \(g(f(x))\) .

text: The composition of \(g : Y \to Z\) with \(f : X \to Y\) is denoted \(g \circ f\) (in Lean, g ∘ f ).

text: Let \(m_1\) and \(m_2\) be integers and suppose that \(q(m_1)=q(m_2)\) . Then by definition \[(m_1+1,2-m_1)=(m_2+1,2-m_2),\] so \(m_1+1=m_2+1\) and \(2-m_1=2-m_2\) , so \(m_1=m_2\) .

• Let \(m_1\) and \(m_2\) be integers and suppose that \(q(m_1)=q(m_2)\). Then by definition

    \[(m_1+1,2-m_1)=(m_2+1,2-m_2),\]

    so \(m_1+1=m_2+1\) and \(2-m_1=2-m_2\), so \(m_1=m_2\).

text: We will show that there exists \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that for all integers \(m\) , \(q(m)\ne(a,b)\) . Indeed, we will show that \((0,1)\) has this property. Suppose that \(m\) were an integer with \(q(m)=(0,1)\) . Then by definition \[(m+1,2-m)=(0,1),\] so \(m+1=0\) and \(2-m=1\) , hence \[\begin{split}1&=(m+1)+(2-m)-2\\ &=0+1-2\\ &=-1,\end{split}\] contradiction.

• We will show that there exists \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) such that for all integers \(m\), \(q(m)\ne(a,b)\). Indeed, we will show that \((0,1)\) has this property. Suppose that \(m\) were an integer with \(q(m)=(0,1)\). Then by definition

    \[(m+1,2-m)=(0,1),\]

    so \(m+1=0\) and \(2-m=1\), hence

    \[\begin{split}1&=(m+1)+(2-m)-2\\ &=0+1-2\\ &=-1,\end{split}\]

    contradiction.

text: Consider the function \(h:\mathbb{R}^3\to \mathbb{R}^3\) defined by, \(h(x,y,z)=(y,z,x)\) . Show that \(h\circ h\circ h=\operatorname{Id}_\mathbb{R}\) . def h : ℝ × ℝ × ℝ → ℝ × ℝ × ℝ | ( x , y , z ) => ( y , z , x ) example : h ∘ h ∘ h = id := by sorry
